dialogue.utterance: 911 to Unit 3 for a reported seizure at 123 Main St in Richmond.
Hi, I'm Mia, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I heard her seizing, and she was unresponsive after it ended.
Does she appear to be opening her eyes at all?
No, not at all.
She isn't responding to loud verbal commands or painful stimuli on her arms or legs.
Her breathing seems irregular-only five breaths per minute and no signs of distress.
Respirations are five per minute-decreased and not labored.
Her skin appearance is normal-no pallor or sweating.
She doesn't make any verbal sounds or follow commands.
Oxygen saturation is at 97% on room air for now.
Her pulse is 98 and consistent-strong and evenly spaced.
Pulse identified as 98 beats per minute and regular.
Blood pressure is 122 over 67. We can keep an eye on that for changes.
Glucose level is 323 mg/dL. That's notable but not the immediate concern.
GCS assessment shows an unresponsive state-3 overall with no eye-opening, no verbal response, and no motor response.
That led to the call for help-anything else you noticed before it happened?
Just laid in bed-she was fine until it happened. No signs of pallor or diaphoresis.
Airway seems patent, no signs of obstruction at this moment.
Inserting an NPA in the left nare to support breathing.
She's still not showing any signs of movement despite external stimuli.
Preparing the BVM mask now-assisted breathing initiated.
Administering first dose of Narcan 0.5mg intranasal. We'll monitor the response.
End-tidal CO2 is reading in the 90s but seems to fluctuate with ventilation.
Starting 4-lead ECG monitoring to document any immediate changes in rhythm.
She currently reports no pain-assessed as zero on a scale.
Now measuring 94 pulse, 15 breaths, and blood pressure at 143/86.
Oxygen saturation has improved to 100% with BVM support.
Current respiration rate is now 15 per minute.
What about earlier this evening? Did she seem off in any way?
She mentioned confusion afterward about events that happened-no recollection of recent.
Any chest pain or dizziness during or after the event?
No chest pain.
Obtained 12-lead ECG for better cardiac assessment-detailed for the ED.
She showed some improvement with increased mental status.
Chippenham is the most suitable hospital for this case-transport recommended.
She's being assisted onto the stretcher. She is now secured for transport.
Current readings: 101 heart rate, 12 breaths per minute, and 122/85 BP.
EKG shows atrial fibrillation with RVR-due to acidosis and involuntary bradycardia.
Small bag of an unknown substance was found-likely a drug overdose instead of a seizure.
Second dose of Narcan given. Response was improved with increased respiratory effort.
CO2 is now gradually dropping with improved breathing-showing response to treatment.
Respiration rate is now 12 per minute, per documentation.
Increasing supplemental O2 to 6L via NC-looks like improvement.
Patient seems stable enough for transfer now-no critical deterioration.
Moving her into the stretcher-secured with straps and rails.
SpO2 held at 93-added 6L supplemental via NC.
IV is being placed at the extremities for medication infusions.
She was unresponsive initially, but now showing signs of confusion and verbal response.
Glucose now at zero post second Narcan dose-documenting in chart.
CO2 is trending down-ventilations effective and stable by two-person assisted BVM.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia and right axis deviation consistent with lab findings.
We are about 11 minutes out from Chippenham.
Switching to two-person BVM to stabilize O2 further and manage Spo2 drop.
End-tidal CO2 improving-some fluctuations but better with setup.
O2 raised to 10L via nasal cannula-Spo2 raised to 96%.
Cardiac arrhythmias resolving as CO2 decreases with BVM support.
Adhering to medical overdose/poisoning-opioid protocol 7-1 throughout treatment.
End-tidal CO2 is steady-documenting with extensive data for ED.
She is now alert but reports no memory and some confusion with events.
12-lead trending as anticipated-moderate right axis in 18-39 female.
Approximately 9 remaining minutes to ambulance arrival at facility.
Vitals steady-no signs of critical decompensation at the moment.
She's been introduced into the stretcher system and integrated to Reeves.
Vitals stable with no acute signs of concern during transport.
SpO2 is 93% and etCO2 remains in mid-90s-consistent with gross observation.
Ventilating efficiently-6L via NC supplemental proving effective.
Rounded off vitals before arrival-SP02 93 and vitals stable.
Pt identified as female, 26 years.
No change in her breathing, no new signs.
ED prepared for ED handoff-arriving in five minutes.
She is stable, with no acute issues during transit.
12-lead ECG obtained and recorded for continuity to handover to Chippenham.
No rashes or bites noted-clear skin consistent with no trauma or infection.
No distension or pain here-everything flat and quiet.
Two-person BVM continued ensuring oxygen delivery with no decrease in etCO2.
EKG remains sinus rhythm with right axis and no new ST segment changes thus far.
No murmurs or irregularities heard so far-no signs of valvular issues.
Her extremity pulses are strong, no swelling, and all joints supple.
Increasing opioid protocol medication-no further Narcan required at this time.
Lung sounds continue to be clear, symmetric, and without new adventitious sounds.
Skin remains non-diaphoretic with warm and dry texture-noted earlier.
She acknowledges verbal prompts and shows limited memory about recent events.
End-tidal CO2 currently in low 80s-stable, but monitored every 2 minutes.
All systems reviewed and no new findings-all interventions confirmed effective.
She knows her name, birth year, and current location-minimally confused otherwise.
Rhythm remains stable, no significant arrhythmias-continued 12-lead ECG monitoring.
IV line is stable and in place for any further interventions if needed.
All medications tracked and ED briefed pre-arrival via radio as per protocol.
Respiration and O2 saturation within acceptable range for acceptable transport epoch.
Updated vitals show no major deviations than prior-we are maintaining steady.
Transport time estimated at three minutes-approaching hospital prep now.
ED has been advised on her condition and pre-hospital treatment-handoff imminent.
She is stable and oriented to self after initial acute phase-alert level increasing.
12-lead shows ongoing mild right axis but no new acute MI or ischemia signs.
She's reporting slight memory of initial moments but not full event-it is normal for her.
No significant change in CO2 since the last measurement-ventilations effectively managed.
She's asking for her phone-showing increased responsiveness and social awareness.
SpO2 remains at 93-documented and stable at time of arrival.
All interventions complete and final vitals documented-prepared for ED handover.
